📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In November 2025, the Lowy Institute published an analysis of the US-Malaysia trade deal (ART), concluding that “Anwar’s international success sours at home.” The analysis identified ‘poison pill’ clauses that function as a “loyalty test” requiring Malaysia to align with US strategic interests — including export controls, sanctions enforcement, and unrestricted critical mineral exports. The Lowy Institute noted that the 19% tariff on Malaysian goods was maintained, not reduced to zero as Anwar’s public framing implied. The deal was characterised as a US strategic win dressed up as a bilateral partnership.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When an independent, respected international think tank describes your trade deal as containing “poison pills” and “loyalty tests,” the government’s spin that this was a diplomatic triumph collapses. The Lowy analysis confirmed what domestic critics had been saying: the ART prioritises US interests over Malaysian sovereignty. The 19% tariff — still among the highest imposed on any US trading partner — was presented domestically as a negotiating win, when in reality it represents a failure to secure the zero-tariff access that competitors like Vietnam achieved.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar framed the trade deal as proof of Malaysia’s elevated international standing. The Lowy Institute framed it as proof that Malaysia got outmanoeuvred. The gap between the government’s narrative and independent analysis is enormous. The “poison pill” clauses — requiring alignment with US export controls and sanctions — effectively subordinate Malaysia’s foreign policy to Washington’s strategic agenda. For a PM who champions non-alignment and Global South solidarity, signing a deal that an international think tank describes as a “loyalty test” is a contradiction that cannot be spun away.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s trade deal history is littered with agreements that looked good in press conferences and bad in practice. The TPPA under Najib was shelved after public fury over sovereignty clauses. The RCEP was signed but implementation has been slow. Each agreement follows the same pattern: government celebrates the signing, analysts identify the problems, public anger builds, and the government pivots to damage control. The ART follows this pattern exactly — except this time, the critics include a respected international institution, not just domestic opposition.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The Lowy Institute called your trade deal a “loyalty test.” Independent analysts identified “poison pill” clauses. The tariff stayed at 19%, not zero. Your own narrative doesn’t match the analysis. Publish the full text of the ART with a clause-by-clause explanation of what Malaysia committed to. Commission an independent economic impact assessment. Explain why Malaysia accepted “loyalty test” provisions that other countries rejected. Or keep celebrating a deal that international analysts describe as a strategic defeat.

Sources: Lowy Institute | MalaysiaNow

item.title: Personal Bankruptcies Rise 5.7% — Self-Declared Bankruptcy Trend 'Growing Concern'
item.description:
Said by: Department of Insolvency Malaysia

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In the first nine months of 2025, Malaysia recorded 4,875 personal bankruptcy cases — a 5.7% increase from the same period in 2024. The Insolvency Department Director-General flagged a “growing concern”: the rise in self-declared bankruptcies, where Malaysians are so financially distressed they voluntarily declare themselves bankrupt rather than waiting for creditors to force the process. The data indicates that financial distress is not concentrated among business owners — it is spreading to salaried workers, young professionals, and gig economy participants.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When people voluntarily declare bankruptcy, it means they have given up hope of recovery. Self-declared bankruptcy is an act of financial surrender — it signals that the individual sees no path to repaying debts through income alone. A 5.7% rise in total cases, combined with a rising self-declared trend, suggests that Malaysia’s cost-of-living crisis is pushing ordinary households past breaking point. Official inflation is 1.3%. But bankruptcies are rising 5.7%. Those two numbers cannot both be telling the truth about the economy.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government celebrates GDP growth, FDI records, and low headline inflation while an increasing number of Malaysians are literally declaring themselves bankrupt. The disconnect between macro indicators and micro reality is the defining feature of the Madani economy: the numbers look good in Putrajaya, but the rakyat is drowning in debt. The bankruptcy threshold was raised from RM50,000 to RM100,000 in 2020 — meaning current figures actually undercount the problem, since people with debts under RM100,000 can no longer be declared bankrupt by creditors. The real financial distress is worse than the data shows.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Personal bankruptcy has been rising in Malaysia for years. Under BN, the government raised the bankruptcy threshold to mask the trend. Under PH 1.0, a moratorium on bankruptcy proceedings was introduced during COVID. Under PH 2.0, the threshold remains at RM100,000 and bankruptcy cases are climbing again. Each government has managed the optics rather than addressing the root causes: stagnant real wages, rising living costs, easy access to consumer credit, and inadequate financial literacy. The cycle continues because addressing it would require admitting that the economy is not working for ordinary people.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Bankruptcies are up 5.7% while you claim inflation is 1.3%. Self-declared bankruptcies are rising — people are so desperate they’re surrendering voluntarily. Your GDP growth is meaningless if it doesn’t translate into household financial security. Expand financial counselling services through AKPK. Regulate predatory lending practices, especially in the buy-now-pay-later sector. Publish household debt-to-income data by income quintile so the rakyat can see the real picture. Or keep quoting macro numbers while more Malaysians walk into the Insolvency Department to declare themselves bankrupt.

Sources: Bernama | Malay Mail | The Star

item.title: Rakyat Says 1.3% Inflation is a Lie — 'Don't Know Where Madanon Gets These Numbers'
item.description:
Said by: Public Sentiment / Social Media

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Malaysia’s official inflation rate for October 2025 was reported at 1.3% by the Department of Statistics (DOSM). Malaysians responded with open mockery on social media, with comments like “don’t know where Madanon gets these numbers.” The disconnect between headline inflation and lived experience is stark: food-away-from-home inflation ran at 4.3-4.7%, far exceeding the headline rate. Rental costs, healthcare, and education expenses — categories that disproportionately affect middle and lower-income households — all rose faster than the official composite figure suggests.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When citizens openly mock their government’s official statistics, it signals a collapse in institutional credibility. The 1.3% headline figure is technically accurate — it measures the CPI basket as defined by DOSM. But the CPI basket doesn’t reflect how ordinary Malaysians actually spend their money. A hawker meal that cost RM5 two years ago now costs RM7-8. Mamak teh tarik has gone from RM1.80 to RM2.50. These are the prices people feel daily — and they bear no resemblance to 1.3%. The gap between official statistics and lived reality erodes public trust in all government data.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government uses the 1.3% figure to claim economic stability while ignoring the composition of that figure. Headline CPI is dragged down by administered prices (fuel subsidies, price controls) that mask underlying inflation. When you subsidise RON95 and control flour and sugar prices, headline inflation looks low — but the moment subsidies are withdrawn, the suppressed inflation hits all at once. The government is not controlling inflation — it is hiding it behind subsidies it can’t afford to maintain. Meanwhile, the items that aren’t price-controlled — food, services, healthcare, education — are rising at 3-5x the headline rate.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian government has used headline inflation figures to paint a rosier picture than reality. Under Najib, the GST introduction in 2015 caused a spike that was partially masked by rebates and handouts. Under Muhyiddin, pandemic-era price controls suppressed official inflation while actual costs soared. The pattern is consistent: governments manipulate the visible number while ordinary households bear invisible cost increases. PH promised transparency and honesty in governance — but its handling of inflation data is indistinguishable from its predecessors.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The rakyat is laughing at your inflation numbers. That should worry you more than the numbers themselves. Publish a “cost of living index” that tracks the actual basket of goods and services used by B40 and M40 households — not the CPI composite that includes imported luxury goods and administered prices. Show Malaysians you understand what they’re actually paying. Or keep quoting 1.3% while mamak meals cost RM8 — and wonder why nobody believes anything you say about the economy.

Sources: DOSM | FMT | Malaysiakini

item.title: Youth Unemployment Stuck at 12.28% — One in Eight Young Malaysians Can't Find Work
item.description:
Said by: DOSM / ILO / World Bank Data

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: As of late 2025, Malaysia’s youth unemployment rate (ages 15-24) remains stuck at 12.28% — roughly 297,500 unemployed young Malaysians. This is three to four times higher than the overall national unemployment rate of 3.2%. DOSM, the ILO, and World Bank data all confirm the structural nature of the problem: Malaysia produces graduates the economy doesn’t need, while industries that are hiring can’t find workers with the right skills. The “Madani economy” that Anwar celebrates apparently doesn’t include young people.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: One in eight young Malaysians can’t find work. This isn’t a cyclical downturn — it’s a structural failure of the education-to-employment pipeline. Universities churn out graduates in fields with limited demand while industries face acute shortages in technical, digital, and skilled trades. The result: a generation of educated young Malaysians who are overqualified for available jobs and underqualified for the jobs that exist. Youth unemployment at 12.28% is a social time bomb — it drives brain drain, delays household formation, increases mental health burdens, and erodes faith in democratic institutions.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government touts GDP growth above 5% and record FDI as proof the economy is thriving. But an economy that grows while leaving one in eight young people unemployed is not thriving — it is extracting value without distributing opportunity. PH promised TVET reform, graduate employability programmes, and industry-aligned education. Three years later, the numbers haven’t moved. The programmes exist on paper. The graduates remain unemployed. The gap between government rhetoric (“Malaysia is booming”) and youth reality (“I can’t find a job”) is the defining economic contradiction of the Anwar era.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Youth unemployment has been structurally elevated in Malaysia for over a decade. Under BN, the rate hovered around 10-11%. Under PH 1.0, it briefly dipped before COVID. Under Muhyiddin and Ismail Sabri, it spiked to 14%+ during the pandemic. Under PH 2.0, it has settled at 12.28% — better than the pandemic peak but worse than pre-pandemic levels. No government has solved it because no government has been willing to reform the education system that produces the mismatch. Universities are political institutions in Malaysia — restructuring them means challenging entrenched interests.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: One in eight young Malaysians can’t find work. Your GDP is growing above 5%. Your FDI is at record highs. And your youth are unemployed. The disconnect is structural — and it’s your responsibility now. Reform TVET funding so it matches industry demand. Publish graduate employability data by university and programme so students can make informed choices. Partner with industries on apprenticeship programmes with guaranteed employment pathways. Or keep celebrating GDP growth while a generation of young Malaysians leaves the country to find work elsewhere.

Sources: DOSM Labour Force Survey | World Bank Malaysia Economic Monitor | ILO

item.title: Zafrul: 'We Had No Choice But to Negotiate' — The Most Honest and Damning Defence of the US Trade Deal
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-29

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Speaking in Parliament on 29 October 2025, Tengku Zafrul delivered what he intended as a defence of the ART but what critics heard as an admission of weakness: ‘We had no choice but to negotiate. Whether we like it or not, we need to defend our access to the US markets, even when under pressure.’ He called the deal the ‘best case scenario’ and accused detractors of ‘ignorance regarding the intense, behind-the-scenes negotiations.’

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: ‘We had no choice’ is the most revealing statement any PH minister has made. It confirms what critics suspected: Malaysia negotiated from a position of weakness, accepted terms it didn’t like, and signed because the alternative — US tariffs — was worse. That may be true. But ‘we had no choice’ is not a defence; it’s an epitaph for Malaysia’s negotiating leverage. When your minister tells Parliament the country had no options, you are telling every future trading partner that Malaysia will take whatever deal is offered.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Other countries had choices. Vietnam, Indonesia, and Thailand all face US tariffs but haven’t signed agreements with the same provisions. Indonesia negotiated its own terms. Vietnam leveraged its manufacturing base. Malaysia, under Zafrul, signed first and defended later. The ’no choice’ framing is particularly damaging because it removes agency — as if Malaysia is a passive recipient of geopolitical forces rather than a sovereign nation with options.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s trade negotiators have historically been among ASEAN’s most skilled. Mahathir — for all his flaws — refused to be pushed around on trade. Even Najib’s TPPA negotiations extracted significant carve-outs. Zafrul’s admission that Malaysia ‘had no choice’ represents a historic low in trade negotiation confidence.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: ‘We had no choice’ is not a reform slogan. It’s a surrender statement. If you truly had no choice, that’s a failure of diplomatic preparation, not a defence of the outcome. The rakyat deserves to know: what choices did you consider and reject? What alternatives were evaluated? Or did you just sign what Washington put in front of you?

Sources: Malay Mail | CNA

item.title: Selangor Water: 27% Lost to Leaks — Amirudin Touts Smart Meters While Pipes Burst
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari, Selangor Menteri Besar

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-27

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 27 October 2025, Amirudin told Parliament that pilot smart water meter projects in Selangor had proven effective — citing Bukit Jelutong where non-revenue water (NRW) was recorded at 27%. He urged the federal government to adopt smart meters nationwide. Separately, the Selangor government announced it would absorb increased water costs for over one million users and accelerate pipe replacement at 300km per year, targeting a 20% water reserve margin by 2030.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Selangor has been PH-governed since 2008. After 17 years, 27% of treated water is still lost to leaks and inefficiencies in a pilot area. Across the state, NRW rates are higher. Air Selangor serves 9.62 million consumers in Selangor, KL, and Putrajaya — the largest water utility in the country. The decades-long failure to fix leaking pipes while water disruptions regularly affect millions of consumers is one of PH’s most tangible governance failures.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Amirudin is lecturing the federal government about smart meters while his own state can’t keep pipes from bursting. Selangor has had repeated water crises — pollution shutdowns, supply disruptions, and rationing — under his watch. The RM320 million Sungai Selangor Alternative Raw Water scheme (SJAM) is being framed as a solution, but the fundamental problem is infrastructure decay that PH has failed to address in 17 years of state government.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The 2023 and 2024 water disruptions in the Klang Valley affected millions and were caused by river pollution shutting down treatment plants — a problem that proper investment in alternative sources would have prevented. Amirudin’s water master plan was only announced in 2025, after 17 years of PH governance.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You’ve governed Selangor’s water infrastructure for 17 years. NRW is still at 27%. Pipes still burst. Treatment plants still shut down from pollution. And your solution is to tell the federal government to buy smart meters? Fix your own pipes first. The rakyat doesn’t need a masterplan announced in year 17 — they needed it in year 1.

Sources: NST | Yahoo News

item.title: Malaysia Committed to Never Banning Rare Earth Exports to the US — Under Zafrul's Trade Deal
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister / USTR

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Under the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade signed by Tengku Zafrul on 26 October 2025, Malaysia committed to not imposing ‘quantitative restrictions’ (bans or quotas) on exports of critical or rare-earth minerals to the United States. The USTR fact sheet explicitly highlights this as a US victory. Meanwhile, Zafrul dismissed Mahathir’s claim that Malaysia agreed to supply unprocessed rare earth at low returns, saying existing policy mandates domestic processing before export.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Rare earth minerals are the 21st century’s most strategic resource — essential for semiconductors, defence systems, EVs, and renewable energy. China controls roughly 60% of global rare earth mining and 90% of processing. The US desperately needs alternative supplies, and Malaysia — with Lynas’s Kuantan plant — is a key node. By committing in writing to never restrict exports to the US, Malaysia surrendered one of its most powerful geopolitical leverage points for nothing more than maintaining the status quo tariff.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Indonesia, Australia, and other mineral-rich nations are using export controls as negotiating leverage to attract downstream processing and technology transfer. Malaysia gave that leverage away. The commitment means that even if a future government wants to use rare earth as a bargaining chip — as China has done effectively — Malaysia is contractually bound not to. Zafrul traded a strategic asset for a tariff rate that didn’t even improve.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: China’s rare earth export restrictions have been its most effective trade weapon for decades. Indonesia’s nickel export ban forced companies to build smelters domestically, creating jobs and value-add. Malaysia had the same opportunity — and signed it away in a trade deal.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You just gave the United States a written guarantee that Malaysia will never use its rare earth resources as leverage. Not for technology transfer. Not for downstream jobs. Not for better trade terms. For nothing. Your minister says Malaysia’s processing-first policy is intact — but the agreement says you can’t restrict exports. When Washington comes knocking for raw minerals, your ‘policy’ will fold against a treaty obligation.

Sources: USTR Fact Sheet | FMT

item.title: Tengku Zafrul Negotiated the US Trade Deal Critics Call 'Worst Agreement Since Independence' — Then Got Promoted
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, former MITI Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 26 October 2025, then-MITI Minister Tengku Zafrul signed the Agreement on Reciprocal Trade (ART) with the United States at the 47th ASEAN Summit in Kuala Lumpur. The deal committed Malaysia to providing preferential market access for US products across chemicals, machinery, vehicles, dairy, poultry, pork, rice, and fuel ethanol. Malaysia also committed to not imposing bans or quotas on the export of critical or rare-earth minerals to the US. In return, the US maintained a 19% reciprocal tariff on Malaysian goods — with only 60.4% of tariff lines zero-rated. Critics from across the political spectrum, including Mahathir and Rafizi Ramli, called it a one-sided surrender.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The USTR fact sheet was published on the White House website before it was shared with Malaysia’s Parliament — meaning Washington told the world what Malaysia agreed to before Malaysia’s own legislators knew. The deal requires Malaysia to mirror US sanctions, act against ‘unfair practices’ by third-country firms (read: China), and open markets in sectors where Malaysian producers are vulnerable. Eurasia Review noted the deal was ‘hastily put together to please the US president during his visit’ and was ’not intended to catch much attention.’ It caught plenty.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Zafrul defended the deal as the ‘best case scenario’ — as if negotiating from weakness is an achievement. He told Parliament: ‘We had no choice but to negotiate.’ That is not a defence; it’s a confession. Malaysia’s neutral foreign policy — maintained for decades — was compromised in one signing ceremony. Zafrul’s reward for this? A promotion to Senior Political Advisor to the PM and appointment as MIDA chairman. In PH’s Malaysia, you get promoted for signing away sovereignty.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has signed trade agreements before without this level of controversy. The CPTPP was negotiated over years with extensive parliamentary scrutiny. The ART was rushed through in weeks, signed at a summit, and defended after the fact. The pattern of PH economic decisions — from 5G dual network to ECRL to this — is consistent: make the politically convenient choice, then spend months defending it.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your minister signed a deal that commits Malaysia to US sanctions compliance and mineral export guarantees, and your defence is ‘we had no choice.’ You always have a choice — you chose to capitulate. Then you promoted the man who did it. If this was really the ‘best case scenario,’ Malaysia’s negotiating position under PH is worse than anyone imagined.

Sources: CNA | USTR | Eurasia Review

item.title: US Trade Deal Details Published on White House Website Before Malaysian Parliament Was Told
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister / White House

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: When the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade was signed on 26 October 2025, the USTR published a detailed fact sheet on the White House website outlining Malaysia’s commitments — including preferential market access for US goods, commitments on rare earth minerals, and tariff schedules. Malaysian MPs and the public learned the details of their own country’s trade agreement from an American government website. The deal’s terms were published in Washington before they were presented in Putrajaya.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A sovereign nation’s trade commitments should be communicated to its own parliament and citizens before they are announced by the other party. The fact that Malaysians had to read the White House website to understand what their government had agreed to is a humiliation of parliamentary sovereignty. It also meant the US controlled the narrative — the terms were framed as an American victory before Malaysia could spin its own story.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH promised transparent governance and parliamentary supremacy. Then Zafrul signed a trade agreement and let Washington publish the terms first. Fahmi Fadzil is the Communications Minister — where was the proactive Malaysian disclosure? The sequence tells you everything about the power dynamics: the US dictated the terms, dictated the timeline, and dictated the narrative. Malaysia’s role was to sign and defend.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s TPPA negotiations under Najib were also criticised for opacity — but at least the government attempted to control its own narrative. Under PH, the narrative was literally ceded to the White House. Eurasia Review noted the deal was ‘hastily put together’ and ’not intended to catch much attention.’ The haste is the point — rushed deals favour the stronger party.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You let the White House tell Malaysians what their own government agreed to. That is not a partnership of equals. That is a relationship where one party writes the press release and the other party nods. If you can’t even control the communications around your own trade deals, how do you expect Malaysians to believe you controlled the negotiations?

Sources: USTR Fact Sheet | Eurasia Review

item.title: US-Malaysia Trade Deal Called 'Worst Agreement Since Independence' and 'Act of Surrender'
item.description:
Said by: Tommy Thomas, Former Attorney General (and critics)

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

The Deal That Sold Malaysia’s Sovereignty

On 26 October 2025, Anwar Ibrahim and Donald Trump signed the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade (ART) at the ASEAN Summit. Former Attorney-General Tommy Thomas called it the worst agreement Malaysia has entered since independence in 1957. He was not exaggerating.

The Facts

Fact: The deal maintains a 19% US tariff on Malaysian goods — down from 25%, but still punitive by any standard. Malaysia’s effective tariff on US goods before this deal was roughly 6%. The US got the better end by a factor of three.

Fact: Malaysia committed to: enforce US tariff evasion rules against third countries, align with US export controls and sanctions regimes, not ban or restrict exports of critical minerals or rare earths to the US, refrain from imposing digital services taxes on US tech companies, and purchase additional American goods and services.

Fact: A termination clause allows the US to reimpose the original 25% tariff if Malaysia enters a trade deal that “jeopardizes essential US interests.” This effectively gives Washington veto power over Malaysia’s independent trade policy — including with China, Malaysia’s largest trading partner.

Fact: The deal’s full text was first published on the White House website before being shared with Malaysian Parliament. Malaysian lawmakers learned the terms of their own trade agreement from a foreign government’s press release.

Fact: Former PM Mahathir said the provisions amounted to “handing over” the country’s independence. Even for a man who oversaw his own share of questionable deals, this one was a bridge too far.

Fact: East Asia Forum’s analysis, titled “The Art of the Deal Lost in US-Malaysia Pact,” argued Malaysia surrendered critical mineral export controls, digital services taxation rights, and independent trade policy in exchange for a tariff cut that still left Malaysia paying three times what the US pays.

Fact: The Guardian reported that Anwar “defended” the deal by arguing it was “the best Malaysia could get under the circumstances” — an argument that concedes the deal is bad while claiming it could have been worse.

Fact: SCMP reported Anwar “slammed” critics for accusing him of surrendering sovereignty — but never addressed the substance of their arguments, only their tone.

Why It Matters

  • This is not a reciprocal deal by any definition of the word. Malaysia makes extensive, binding concessions across minerals, digital taxation, sanctions alignment, and procurement. The US reduces a punitive tariff by 6 percentage points and retains the right to reimpose it at will. In any negotiation textbook, this is called capitulation.
  • The sovereignty clause is the most dangerous provision. If Malaysia wants to deepen trade ties with China — its largest trading partner — the US can terminate the ART and reimpose 25% tariffs. Malaysia has effectively given Washington a veto over its foreign economic policy.
  • Publishing the details on the White House website before informing Parliament is not just embarrassing — it is a constitutional affront. Trade agreements that constrain national sovereignty should require parliamentary debate and approval before signing. Anwar bypassed the legislature on the most consequential economic agreement in decades.
  • Every ASEAN neighbour is watching. Indonesia walked away from a similar US proposal. Vietnam negotiated harder. Thailand delayed. Malaysia signed first and asked questions later. This weakens Malaysia’s negotiating position in every future bilateral and multilateral trade discussion.
  • The “best we could get” defence is an admission of weakness, not a justification. If this was truly the best outcome, it means Malaysia’s diplomatic leverage is weaker than the government has claimed — which raises questions about the entire Madani foreign policy brand. *Sources: The Guardian · East Asia Forum · SCMP · *Straits Times

item.title: 77% of Malaysians Struggle With Rising Living Costs — But Anwar Says Economy Is 'Strong'
item.description:
Said by: Consumer Frontiers International survey

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-15

The 77% vs The GDP

In October 2025, a survey found that 77% of Malaysians said they struggled with rising living costs. In the same month, the government was citing GDP growth and investment figures as evidence that the economy was strong.

The Facts

Fact: Consumer Frontiers International found 77% of Malaysians struggled with rising costs despite rising wages.

Fact: DOSM reported average monthly disposable household income increased 3.2% to RM7,584 in 2024.

Fact: But economist Lee Heng Guie warned: “We must also recognise that the cost of living continues to rise.” Real gains were being eroded by inflation.

Fact: The Star headlined it: “Rising cost of living dampens income gains.”

Why It Matters

  • GDP growth and approved investments are macro numbers. They do not reflect what 77% of the population is experiencing.
  • When three-quarters of your citizens say they’re struggling, citing investment figures is tone-deaf.
  • The gap between government statistics and lived experience is the gap that creates protest movements. *Sources: Business Today · *The Star

item.title: Gig Economy Workers Still Without Protections — Grab, Foodpanda Riders Risk Lives for Poverty Wages
item.description:
Said by: Labour Advocates / i-Saraan Programme

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-10

Gig workers remain without basic labour protections — no minimum wage, no mandatory accident insurance, no collective bargaining. Government’s i-Saraan Plus offers voluntary EPF matching of up to RM600/year, a band-aid on a gaping wound.

item.title: Tax Revenue Collection Efficiency Remains Poor — Shadow Economy Estimated at 20-30% of GDP
item.description:
Said by: Ministry of Finance / IMF Estimates

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-01

Shadow economy estimated at 20-30% of GDP. PH abolished GST in 2018 and never replaced it with an equally effective broad-based tax. The result: chronic structural revenue shortfall forcing RM75B in annual borrowing.

item.title: RON95 Subsidy Savings Slashed — From RM8 Billion Target to Actual RM950 Million
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim / Reuters

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-09-23

RON95 subsidy savings target slashed three times: RM8B to RM4B to RM950M actual. Barely 12% of the original target. The government spent more political capital debating the reform than it saved in ringgit.

item.title: EPF Account 3 Raid: RM14.79 Billion Withdrawn — Retirement Savings Hemorrhaging
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister & Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-08-14

4.63 million contributors (35% of EPF members under 55) withdrew RM14.79B from the Flexible Account since May 2024. EPF warns 6 million members have less than RM10,000 saved. Solving today’s cost-of-living crisis by creating tomorrow’s retirement crisis.

item.title: MITI Accused of Stealing World Expo Osaka Pavilion Concept — Creative Agency Serves Letter of Demand
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister / Current Media Group

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-08-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In August 2025, MITI under Tengku Zafrul was served a letter of demand by Current Media Group (CMG), a creative consultancy that claimed the ministry stole its concept for Malaysia’s World Expo 2025 Osaka pavilion without providing recognition or payment. The allegation was that CMG’s original pitch was used as the basis for the pavilion design without a contract or credit. Zafrul addressed the matter in a parliamentary reply to Kepong MP Lim Lip Eng.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: If a government ministry used a private company’s intellectual property without payment or credit, that is theft — regardless of whether it’s called ‘inspiration’ or ‘parallel development.’ World Expo pavilions are high-profile national showcases with significant budgets. The allegation that MITI took a concept without paying for it raises serious questions about procurement integrity and intellectual property respect within the ministry.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The ‘reformist’ government that lectures the world about good governance and fair dealing allegedly stole a creative concept from a Malaysian company. PH came to power promising transparent procurement and respect for due process. If MITI can’t even commission a pavilion design without a letter of demand from the original creator, what does that say about how other, larger contracts are handled behind closed doors?

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Government IP theft from private contractors is a recurring complaint in Malaysia. Creative agencies, architects, and consultants have long reported that government pitches are treated as free work — ideas are taken, contracts go elsewhere, and the original creators are ghosted. Under BN, this was standard. Under PH, it was supposed to change.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You represent Malaysia on the world stage at Osaka and you can’t even pay the people who designed it? If the allegation is true, compensate CMG and investigate how this happened. If it’s false, release the procurement documents. Transparency works both ways — you don’t get to lecture the world about governance while your own ministry is served letters of demand for IP theft.

Sources: Marketing Interactive

item.title: Tens of Thousands March Demanding Anwar Resign Over Cost of Living — First Major Protest Since GE15
item.description:
Said by: Opposition parties and protesters

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-07-26

The Streets Speak

On 26 July 2025, tens of thousands of Malaysians marched to Merdeka Square in Kuala Lumpur, demanding Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim step down. The rally — the first major protest since GE15 — was driven by rising costs and broken reform promises.

The Facts

Fact: Al Jazeera reported thousands gathered at Independence Square, calling for Anwar to resign.

Fact: Bloomberg’s headline was blunt: “Anwar’s Broken Promises Spark Protests Demanding His Ouster.”

Fact: The rally was organized by opposition parties but attracted broad public participation driven by cost of living anger.

Fact: By October 2025, a survey found 77% of Malaysians said they struggled with rising living costs.

Fact: Just days before the protest, Anwar announced a RM100 cash payment to all adult citizens and a further RON95 price cut — widely seen as a pre-emptive measure.

Why It Matters

  • When tens of thousands march against a government that promised “keluarga Malaysia” and reform, it is not a fringe protest. It is a verdict.
  • The RM100 handout was political triage — a band-aid on structural economic pain.
  • 77% struggling with cost of living. That is not an opposition narrative. That is a national reality. *Sources: Al Jazeera · Bloomberg · *Business Today

item.title: Bumiputera Policy 'Non-Negotiable' in US Trade Talks — Anwar Defends Race-Based Economics
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-07-21

Anwar declared Bumiputera policy ’non-negotiable’ in US tariff talks, saying ’no country should interfere with domestic decisions.’ Critics argue race-based policies are the very market distortion the US objects to.

item.title: Electricity Tariffs Hiked for Industry on Same Day as SST Expansion — 'Hotpot of Bad News'
item.description:
Said by: Ministry of Finance

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-07-01

The Hotpot of Bad News

On 1 July 2025, Malaysia was hit by a double whammy: the expanded SST took effect, covering rental, healthcare, education, and more, alongside increased electricity tariffs for the industrial sector. The Straits Times called it a “hotpot of bad economic news.”

The Facts

Fact: SST expansion took effect 1 July 2025, taxing six new service categories including rental, construction, financial services, private healthcare, and education.

Fact: On the same day, electricity tariffs increased for the industrial sector.

Fact: The government was also planning to reduce cooking gas subsidies for commercial eateries and implement the RON95 subsidy reform by year end.

Fact: The Straits Times noted the “widening of the SST is just the latest controversial move” by the government.

Why It Matters

  • Any one of these measures is manageable. All of them on the same timeline is a cost of living assault.
  • The cumulative effect on small businesses is devastating: higher tax, higher electricity, higher fuel, and reduced subsidies.
  • The government says each measure is “necessary.” But necessity without sequencing is still bad governance. *Source: *Straits Times

item.title: Rafizi Ramli Forced Out of Economy Ministry — Anwar Says 'No Need to Reshuffle Anything'
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-06-16

The Economy Minister Leaves and the PM Shrugs

In June 2025, Rafizi Ramli — arguably the most data-driven minister in Anwar’s cabinet — resigned as Economy Minister after losing the PKR deputy president race to Nurul Izzah. Anwar’s response was breathtakingly casual.

The Facts

Fact: Rafizi resigned from cabinet on 16 June 2025, following his defeat in the PKR party elections.

Fact: Anwar told reporters: “I do not see the need to reshuffle anything so far. I wish all the best to Rafizi.”

Fact: The Economy Ministry portfolio remained vacant for six months until the December 2025 reshuffle, when Akmal Nasrullah was appointed.

Why It Matters

  • The Economy Ministry is not a minor portfolio. Leaving it vacant for half a year while managing subsidy reform, cost of living pressures, and fiscal consolidation is negligence.
  • Anwar’s casual dismissal suggests the real economy was never as important as the political economy — meaning coalition management and election positioning.
  • Rafizi has since emerged as an internal critic, warning that Malaysia “can no longer afford” to delay reforms. SCMP asked in February 2026 whether Rafizi is now “the conscience of the reformasi movement” — a title Anwar once held. *Sources: Bloomberg · SCMP · *Reuters

item.title: EPF Retirement Crisis — Average Balance of RM47,600 at Age 55 Means POVERTY in Old Age
item.description:
Said by: EPF Data / Retirement Adequacy Studies

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-06-01

📌 What Happened

EPF data consistently shows that the majority of Malaysians have grossly inadequate retirement savings. The median balance at age 55 (mandatory withdrawal age) is approximately RM47,600 — enough to last roughly 5 years at RM800/month, well below any reasonable poverty line. The government’s policy of allowing repeated EPF withdrawals (Account 3 saw RM14.79 billion withdrawn by 4.63 million contributors) has accelerated retirement fund depletion.

🔥 Why It Matters

Malaysia has no comprehensive social safety net for retirees outside of EPF (for private sector) and pensions (for civil servants). When EPF runs dry — which it does for most retirees within 5-10 years — elderly Malaysians face destitution. There is no universal old-age pension, inadequate public aged care facilities, and limited social assistance. A generation of Malaysians is heading toward retirement poverty.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Instead of strengthening retirement savings, the government has repeatedly raided EPF for short-term political wins. Account 3 — the latest withdrawal scheme — was presented as ‘flexibility’ but in reality it’s the government allowing citizens to spend their future to survive the present. When you can’t fix wages, cost of living, or employment, the politically easy answer is ’let them eat their retirement.’ It’s not a solution — it’s kicking the catastrophe 20 years down the road.

📜 Previous Cases

EPF withdrawals during COVID-19 (under PN/BN) depleted RM145 billion from the fund. PH criticised those withdrawals as irresponsible. Then PH introduced Account 3 with even easier withdrawal terms. Every government — BN, PN, PH — has treated EPF as a piggy bank for political convenience. The retirees of 2040 will pay the price.

🎯 PH Callout

You criticised Muhyiddin for allowing COVID EPF withdrawals. Then you created Account 3, making it even EASIER to withdraw retirement savings. RM14.79 billion gone in months. You’ve traded the financial security of 4.63 million Malaysians’ retirement for short-term political approval. When these people are 70 and destitute, they’ll remember who let them spend their old age today.

Sources

  • EPF — Belanjawan 2026
  • CNA — Malaysia Development Plan

item.title: FDI Announcements vs Actual Jobs — RM Numbers Look Great, But Where Are the Jobs for Malaysians?
item.description:
Said by: MIDA / Economic Analysts

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-06-01

📌 What Happened

The government regularly announces massive Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) figures — billions of ringgit in commitments from tech giants, manufacturers, and data centre operators. These announcements generate impressive headlines. However, the gap between announced FDI and actual realised investment, and between investment and actual Malaysian employment, remains significant.

🔥 Why It Matters

FDI announcements are easy PR wins — you stand next to a foreign CEO, sign an MOU, and announce billions. But approved FDI ≠ realised FDI (many projects are delayed or cancelled). Realised FDI ≠ Malaysian jobs (many investments are capital-intensive with minimal employment, or employ foreign specialists). And the jobs that are created are often not the high-quality, well-paying positions that address Malaysia’s unemployment and wage stagnation challenges.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Data centres — the crown jewel of FDI announcements — employ remarkably few people relative to their investment size. A billion-ringgit data centre might employ 50-200 permanent staff, mostly technical specialists. Compare that to a factory employing thousands. The government promotes FDI numbers that look impressive on paper while youth unemployment stays at 12.28% and graduate unemployment remains persistent. The headline numbers don’t translate to the lives of ordinary Malaysians.

📜 Previous Cases

BN also regularly announced massive FDI figures while employment quality stagnated. Najib’s Economic Transformation Programme produced impressive investment statistics but failed to meaningfully raise median wages or reduce inequality. PH promised that their FDI strategy would be different — more quality, more jobs, more technology transfer. The strategy is identical: announce big numbers, hope nobody checks the employment data.

🎯 PH Callout

Stop announcing FDI commitments as if they’re achievements. Announce JOBS. Announce WAGES. Announce how many Malaysians got employed, at what salary, in what roles. A billion ringgit in FDI that creates 100 jobs is not an economic success story — it’s a tax incentive giveaway that benefits foreign shareholders while Malaysians deliver their food on motorcycles for RM6 per trip.

Sources

  • ISEAS — Bumiputera Transformation 2035
  • Bloomberg — Malaysia Debt Pressure

item.title: Foreign Labour Dependency Trap — Economy Can't Function Without Workers Malaysia Treats Like Disposable Parts
item.description:
Said by: Labour Economists / ILO

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-06-01

📌 What Happened

Malaysia remains heavily dependent on foreign workers — estimated at 2-3 million documented and possibly millions more undocumented — in critical sectors including construction, manufacturing, agriculture, and services. The government’s foreign worker policy oscillates between liberalisation and restriction based on political winds rather than economic planning, creating chronic uncertainty for both employers and workers.

🔥 Why It Matters

Foreign worker dependency reveals Malaysia’s failure to move up the value chain — the economy is still structurally reliant on cheap, exploitable labour rather than automation, technology, and skilled domestic workers. This dependency suppresses local wages, enables exploitation, and creates social tensions.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Every government promises to ‘reduce dependency on foreign labour’ and ‘move to a high-value economy.’ Every government then allows millions of foreign workers because the industries that fund political parties can’t function without them. Palm oil plantations, construction companies, and factories NEED cheap foreign labour. Politicians NEED campaign funding from these industries. The result is a permanent cycle of promised reform and actual dependency.

📜 Previous Cases

BN’s foreign worker policies were equally chaotic and exploitation-enabling. The US State Department’s Trafficking in Persons report has flagged Malaysia’s forced labour issues for years — under BN, PN, and PH. The multi-tier foreign worker levy system, repeatedly delayed, was supposed to be PH’s solution. It wasn’t.

🎯 PH Callout

Your economy literally can’t build a building, harvest a palm oil fruit, or run a restaurant without foreign workers — workers who have no rights, no protections, and no voice. Your ‘Madani’ framework apparently doesn’t extend to the millions of human beings who actually make your economy function. Fix the exploitation, fix the wages, fix the dependency — or stop pretending Malaysia is a civilised economy.

Sources

  • ISEAS — Petrol Subsidy Reform
  • Eurasia Review — Race-Based Economic Policies

item.title: Mandatory Halal Certification Burden on Non-Muslim SMEs — Pay Up or Close Down
item.description:
Said by: SME Owners / JAKIM

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-06-01

📌 What Happened

JAKIM’s expanding halal certification requirements impose significant compliance costs on food businesses, including those owned by non-Muslims. Businesses must pay for certification, comply with extensive requirements covering ingredients, preparation, storage, and staffing, and undergo regular audits. JAKIM’s budget of RM2.6 billion makes it one of the best-funded agencies in government — better funded than many agencies that directly serve all Malaysians.

🔥 Why It Matters

For small non-Muslim food businesses — Chinese coffee shops, Indian restaurants, mixed-cuisine eateries — halal certification requirements create a financial and operational burden that can be prohibitive. The choice becomes: spend thousands on halal compliance (in a business that may not even serve a majority Muslim clientele) or lose access to government contracts, events, and certain commercial spaces that require halal certification.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

JAKIM receives RM2.6 billion annually — more than the sports budget, more than environmental protection, and disproportionate to its actual contribution to GDP or social welfare. The expanding certification regime functions as a religious tax on business, with the costs ultimately passed to consumers. For a PM who champions a ‘moderate’ image internationally, the growth of JAKIM’s power and budget under his watch tells a different story domestically.

📜 Previous Cases

The KK Mart controversy in 2024 — where a convenience store chain was boycotted and faced criminal charges for inadvertently selling socks with an Allah-related design — showed how religious enforcement can destroy businesses over minor incidents. Under PH, religious agencies have not been reined in; they’ve been emboldened.

🎯 PH Callout

Your ‘Madani’ government spends RM2.6 billion on JAKIM while healthcare crumbles and schools produce students who can’t do math. Non-Muslim businesses subsidise a religious bureaucracy through mandatory compliance costs. If ‘Malaysia Madani’ means all Malaysians, why does budget allocation suggest it means only one community’s religious infrastructure?

Sources

  • CNA — Anwar Faces Graft Crisis
  • Eurasia Review — Race-Based Economic Policies

item.title: Ringgit Weakness Persists Despite GDP Growth — Currency Doesn't Believe Anwar's Economic Story
item.description:
Said by: BNM / Currency Markets

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-06-01

📌 What Happened

Despite the government’s optimistic GDP growth narrative, the Malaysian ringgit has remained persistently weak against the US dollar and regional currencies, hovering around historically low levels. The currency markets — which aggregate the collective judgment of thousands of investors and analysts — are essentially voting no-confidence on Malaysia’s economic fundamentals.

🔥 Why It Matters

The weak ringgit has direct consequences for ordinary Malaysians: imported goods become more expensive (contributing to the food inflation that rakyat experience but official statistics undercount), overseas education costs more, and the brain drain accelerates as Malaysian professionals find their skills worth multiples more in stronger-currency countries.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The government has pointed to global factors including US monetary policy and geopolitical uncertainty, and these are real factors. But regional comparisons reveal that the ringgit has underperformed several ASEAN peers, suggesting Malaysia-specific factors are at play: political uncertainty, the MACC scandal, concern about fiscal sustainability with 64.6% debt-to-GDP, and questions about the credibility of economic reforms. Currency markets are brutally honest barometers of national confidence. The ringgit’s weakness tells a story the government’s GDP figures don’t.

🎯 PH Callout

You can cherry-pick GDP numbers for press conferences. But you can’t spin the forex market. The ringgit doesn’t care about your talking points — it responds to fundamentals. And right now, the fundamentals say the market doesn’t believe your economic story. Fix the substance, and the currency will follow. Keep spinning, and it won’t.

Sources

  • Bloomberg — Anwar on Debt Pressure

item.title: SMEs Struggle to Access Loans — Banks Prefer Low-Risk Lending While Government Claims 'Business-Friendly'
item.description:
Said by: SME Association / Bank Negara Data

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-06-01

SMEs employ 7M+ workers and contribute 40% of GDP but face significant barriers accessing financing. Banks remain risk-averse, loan rejection rates high. SMEs face a perfect storm: rising costs from SST, labour shortages, and slow-paying government agencies.

item.title: Malaysians Pay Highest Grocery Bills in Southeast Asia — So Much for 'Madani Economy'
item.description:
Said by: USDA Economic Research Service / DOSM

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-05-29

USDA data: Malaysia’s food expenditure highest in SE Asia at US$1,940/person/year. What cost RM30.90 in 1980 now costs RM100. B40 households spend 71.6% of income on daily necessities.

item.title: Purchasing Power Collapsed: What Cost RM30.90 in 1980 Now Costs RM100
item.description:
Said by: DOSM / Malaysian Statistics Department

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-04-01

69.1% decline in purchasing power for food since 1980 — what cost RM30.90 then costs RM100 now. B40 households spend 71.6% of income on daily necessities. Official inflation doesn’t match kitchen-table reality.

item.title: Sapura Energy RM1.1 Billion Bailout — Anwar Says It's 'Not a Bailout' With a Straight Face
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-03-13

Government injected RM1.1B into debt-laden Sapura Energy despite Anwar previously criticising bailout culture. He denied it was a bailout. CNA called it a ‘political bind.’ MACC later probed the company. Sources: https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/se-asia/malaysias-pm-anwar-denies-bailout-claims-after-pumping-s330-million-into-ailing-sapura-energy | https://www.scmp.com/week-asia/politics/article/3302641/anger-malaysia-over-anwars-bailout-sapura-energy-mounts-amid-corruption-probe

item.title: Immigration Corruption Syndicates Thrive — Foreign Worker Exploitation a Multi-Billion Ringgit Industry
item.description:
Said by: Enforcement Agencies / Media Investigations

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-03-01

📌 What Happened

Malaysia’s immigration system remains plagued by corruption syndicates that exploit millions of foreign workers, extracting billions of ringgit through illegal levies, document fraud, and labour trafficking. Despite repeated government promises to clean up the system, investigations continue to reveal deeply entrenched networks involving immigration officials, labour agents, and employers.

🔥 Why It Matters

Foreign workers — numbering an estimated 2-3 million documented and millions more undocumented — pay exorbitant recruitment fees, often becoming indentured servants trapped in cycles of debt bondage. The multi-tier foreign worker levy system has been delayed repeatedly, creating uncertainty that benefits corrupt intermediaries. US State Department Trafficking in Persons reports have consistently flagged Malaysia’s forced labour issues, particularly in plantation agriculture, construction, and manufacturing.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

The government’s response cycle is predictable: announce a crackdown, conduct a few high-profile raids, issue stern statements, then allow the system to return to business as usual. Immigration corruption is a structural problem requiring systemic reform of recruitment processes, levy collection, and worker protection — not periodic enforcement theatre.

🎯 PH Callout

You inherited a broken system and promised to fix it. Instead, the syndicates still operate, the workers still suffer, and the billions still flow to corrupt intermediaries. If you can’t clean up your own immigration department, stop pretending you’re a reform government.

Sources

  • ISEAS — Malaysia’s Petrol Subsidy Reform

item.title: Minimum Wage RM1,700 — Workers Still Can't Bargain, Employers Defer 6 Months
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, PM / Ministry of Human Resources

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-02-01

RM1,700 minimum wage came into effect Feb 2025 but only for companies with 5+ employees. Full enforcement deferred to Aug 2025. Fulcrum noted ‘most workers still lack bargaining power.’

item.title: MAHB Privatisation — Independent Directors Say 'Not Fair' But Proceed Anyway
item.description:
Said by: Gateway Development Alliance / Khazanah

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-01-24

MAHB’s own independent directors rejected the RM11/share takeover as ‘unfair and unreasonable.’ Civil society protested BlackRock’s GIP involvement amid Palestine boycott concerns. MAHB delisted anyway.

item.title: Festive Season Toll-Free Travel Discontinued — Even This Small Perk Gone
item.description:
Said by: Government of Malaysia

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-01-21

Festive toll-free travel quietly discontinued. PH promised to abolish tolls entirely in GE15. Instead, tolls still stand, government pays RM591M to freeze existing rates, and now even the small festive perk is gone.

item.title: Brain Drain Accelerating — 1.86 Million Malaysians Have Left, and the Best Keep Leaving
item.description:
Said by: World Bank / Talent Corporation Data

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-01-01

1.86 million Malaysians living abroad, disproportionately highly skilled. Public hospital doctors work 84-hour weeks for poverty wages. 11,000 specialist shortage directly linked to brain drain. Structural causes — race-based policies, low wages, poor governance — remain unaddressed.

item.title: Civil Servants Get Pensions-for-Life, Private Workers Get Depleting EPF — The Two-Tier Retirement System
item.description:
Said by: Public Finance Analysts / EPF

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-01-01

1.3-1.6M civil servants get defined-benefit pensions for life funded by taxpayers. 16M private sector workers rely on EPF with median balance of RM47,600 that runs out in 5 years. No government has had the courage to reform it because the civil service is a critical vote bank.

item.title: Proton Still Dependent on Government Protections — 40 Years of 'National Car' and Still Can't Compete
item.description:
Said by: Automotive Industry Analysts

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-01-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: After 40 years and billions in public subsidies, tax exemptions, and protectionist trade policies, Proton still cannot compete internationally without government protection. High import duties — excise duties of up to 100% on foreign cars — inflate vehicle prices for 33 million Malaysians. The same car costs half as much in Thailand or Indonesia. Anwar’s government shows no inclination to reform automotive trade policies or phase out protections that cost Malaysian consumers billions annually.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Car ownership is not a luxury in Malaysia — it is a necessity in a country with inadequate public transport outside KL. High import duties mean Malaysian families spend a disproportionate share of their income on vehicles. A Honda City that costs RM65,000 in Thailand costs over RM100,000 in Malaysia. A Toyota Corolla in Indonesia costs about RM80,000; in Malaysia, over RM130,000. The “national car” policy forces 33 million people to subsidise one company’s inability to compete — through higher prices on every vehicle they buy.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Proton was supposed to be a temporary infant industry — protected for a few years until it could stand on its own. Forty years later, it still can’t stand on its own. The company was sold to Geely (China) in 2017 because it couldn’t survive without a foreign partner. Yet import duties remain, protecting a company that is no longer even fully Malaysian-owned. The protection no longer serves a national champion — it serves a joint venture with a Chinese automaker while Malaysian consumers pay inflated prices for every car on the road.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The national car project has been a political sacred cow since Mahathir launched Proton in 1983. Every government has maintained protections because the automotive lobby — dealers, suppliers, workers — represents votes. BN protected Proton for 35 years. PH 1.0 made no changes. PH 2.0 has made no changes. The policy survives not because it works but because dismantling it would anger connected interests. Meanwhile, Thailand and Indonesia — without national car protections — have become major automotive manufacturing hubs. Malaysia has not.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Malaysian families pay double for cars because of a 40-year-old policy that protects a company now half-owned by China. Public transport is inadequate. Car ownership is mandatory. And you maintain import duties that inflate prices for everyone. Phase out automotive protections over 5-10 years. Invest the savings in public transport. Let Malaysian consumers buy cars at regional prices. Or keep forcing 33 million people to subsidise a failed industrial policy — and wonder why cost of living is your biggest political problem.

Sources: Paultan.org | Nikkei Asia | The Edge

item.title: Nga Kor Ming Blows RM4 Million on 4 'BMW-Class Smart Toilets' While Rakyat Struggles With Cost of Living
item.description:
Said by: Nga Kor Ming, Housing and Local Government Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-11-14

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In November 2024, Housing and Local Government Minister Nga Kor Ming announced that RM4 million had been allocated to build four “BMW-class” smart toilets in Kuala Lumpur and Putrajaya — RM1 million per toilet. He made the announcement at the Toilet of the Year Award (TOTYA) 2024 ceremony, describing it as part of a broader RM180 million allocation over three years for public toilet upgrades. By June 2025, former MCA vice-president Ti Lian Ker demanded an update, noting zero progress reports since the announcement.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: RM1 million per toilet. The average Malaysian household income is around RM7,000-8,000 per month. The government is spending the equivalent of over 10 years of a household’s income on a single toilet, while telling the rakyat to tighten their belts. The RM180 million national toilet upgrade is a legitimate use of funds. But turning four toilets into “BMW-class” showpieces at RM1 million each — in KL and Putrajaya, the places that already have the best infrastructure — is the kind of tone-deaf spending that makes people lose faith in reform governments.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Nga Kor Ming is the same minister who threatened a journalist for asking about event naming, and whose MyKiosk programme failed MACC review for lacking a needs assessment. “BMW-class” toilets — the branding alone tells you this isn’t about serving the public, it’s about making the minister look good. And when asked for an update months later? Silence. No progress report. No accountability. Just a vanity announcement followed by a black hole of implementation. Meanwhile, regular public toilets across Malaysia remain filthy and broken. But sure, build luxury toilets in areas that already have the best infrastructure.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a long history of inflated government procurement. The RM600 toilet seats and RM1,200 screwdrivers of the BN era were bread-and-butter corruption stories. PH screamed about wasteful spending for decades. Now they’re spending RM1 million per toilet and calling it innovation. Nga himself has a pattern of grand announcements with poor follow-through — the MyKiosk programme being the most recent example of MACC flagging his ministry’s projects for governance failures.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You promised fiscal discipline and responsible governance. You told Malaysians every ringgit would be spent wisely under Kerajaan Madani. Then you spend RM1 million per toilet and can’t provide a progress report when asked. The rakyat can’t afford eggs, but your minister is building BMW toilets. If this is reform, the rakyat would like a refund.

Sources: FMT | NST | Bernama

item.title: Rice Shortages Deepen Food Security Fears — Anwar Wants 'More Monitoring'
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late 2024, sporadic rice shortages hit Malaysia alongside devastating floods. Supermarket shelves emptied of local rice varieties in multiple states. Anwar’s response was to call for “frequent monitoring and enforcement” — the government’s default when it has no plan. Malaysia imports over 30% of its rice supply, and the domestic padi industry has been neglected for decades. Global rice prices hit multi-year highs due to India’s export restrictions, El Niño impacts on Southeast Asian harvests, and supply chain disruptions.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Rice is the staple food for 33 million Malaysians. When rice shelves are empty, food security is not theoretical — it is immediate. Malaysia’s 30%+ import dependency means any global supply disruption directly threatens domestic food security. The padi industry has been underfunded, fragmented, and politically managed for decades. Smallholder farmers receive insufficient support. Irrigation infrastructure is crumbling. Yields per hectare lag behind Thailand, Vietnam, and Indonesia. The rice shortage was predictable — and the government was not prepared.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: “Monitoring and enforcement” is what you do when you have no plan. It is the bureaucratic equivalent of watching the problem get worse while looking busy. PH promised food security reform. Three years later, Malaysia’s rice import dependency hasn’t decreased, domestic production hasn’t meaningfully increased, and the government’s response to shortages is enforcement — punishing traders for hoarding rather than addressing the structural supply deficit. You can’t enforce rice into existence. You need to grow it, and that requires investment the government hasn’t made.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has faced periodic rice shortages under every government. Under BN, the response was always the same: release strategic reserves, blame hoarders, promise investment in padi. Under PH 1.0, food security was briefly elevated as a policy priority before the Sheraton Move. Under PH 2.0, the government launched a “food security” initiative that generated press conferences but no measurable improvement in self-sufficiency ratios. The structural problem — declining padi acreage, aging farmers, insufficient irrigation, and reliance on imports — remains identical to what it was 20 years ago.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Rice shelves are empty. Your response is “monitoring.” Malaysia imports 30%+ of its rice and you’ve done nothing to reduce that dependency in three years. Invest in padi infrastructure — irrigation, mechanisation, yield improvement. Support smallholder farmers with guaranteed minimum prices. Build strategic reserves that can absorb global supply shocks. Or keep “monitoring” while Malaysians can’t find rice at the supermarket — and hope they don’t notice that “monitoring” is not food security.

Sources: Bernama | FMT | Reuters

item.title: Foreign Worker Levy Multi-Tier System — Announced 2023, Still Not Fully Implemented
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-10-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In September 2023, Anwar announced a multi-tier levy system for foreign workers designed to reduce Malaysia’s dependency on imported labour. The system was repeated in Budget 2025 with further details. The Malaysian Employers Federation (MEF) complained the system was still unfair and insufficiently implemented. Anwar’s promise to cut foreign worker dependency to 15% of the workforce by 2025 was not met. As of late 2025, Malaysia still employed approximately 2.2 million documented foreign workers, with an estimated 1-2 million undocumented — a dependency that has not meaningfully changed under PH.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Foreign worker dependency suppresses local wages, particularly for B40 Malaysians who compete directly with imported labour in construction, agriculture, manufacturing, and services. The multi-tier levy was supposed to make it progressively more expensive for employers to hire foreign workers, incentivising automation, productivity improvements, and local hiring. Two years after announcement, the system is still not fully implemented. Meanwhile, industries continue importing workers at levels that undercut the wage floor for Malaysian citizens.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Announcing a policy and implementing it are two different things — and PH excels at the former while failing at the latter. The multi-tier levy was announced in 2023, repeated in 2024, and included in Budget 2025. At each stage, it was presented as a new initiative. The 15% target was quietly dropped when it became clear it wouldn’t be met. MEF’s complaints suggest that even the implemented portions are poorly designed. The foreign worker lobby — representing plantation companies, construction firms, and manufacturers with political connections — has successfully delayed meaningful reform while the announcements continue.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian government has promised to reduce foreign worker dependency. Under Mahathir 1.0, the promise was made in the 1990s. Under Najib, various levy increases were announced. Under PH 1.0, a freeze on new foreign worker permits was briefly implemented before being reversed. Under PH 2.0, the multi-tier levy was announced with fanfare but implementation has been glacial. The pattern is consistent: announce reform, face industry pushback, delay implementation, announce it again in the next budget. Meanwhile, 3-4 million foreign workers remain in the country.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You announced the multi-tier levy in 2023. It’s 2026 and it’s still not fully implemented. Your 15% target was missed. MEF says the system is unfair. Industries keep importing workers at the same rate. Either implement the levy with teeth — real enforcement, meaningful cost increases for employers who over-rely on foreign labour — or admit that the announcement was for headlines, not for policy. Malaysian workers deserve local wage protection. They’re not getting it because your government is more afraid of upsetting the plantation lobby than protecting the rakyat.

Sources: Bernama | FMT | MEF

item.title: Forest City Special Financial Zone — 0% Tax for the Ultra-Rich While Rakyat Pays 8% SST
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim Administration / Securities Commission

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-10-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In October 2024, the PH government launched the Forest City Special Financial Zone (SFZ) in Johor, offering 0% corporate tax for up to 15 years for qualifying businesses, 0% tax for ultra-high-net-worth family offices, and incentives for fintech, fund management, and digital asset companies. The SFZ was designed to rescue Forest City — a RM400 billion Chinese-backed property development that sat largely empty — by transforming it into a financial hub. Meanwhile, ordinary Malaysians pay 8% SST on everyday services including healthcare, education, and rent.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The SFZ creates a two-tier tax system: 0% for the ultra-rich who park their wealth in Forest City, and 8% SST for ordinary Malaysians buying groceries, seeing a doctor, or sending their children to school. The optics are devastating — the government is simultaneously expanding SST to cover basic necessities while offering tax-free status to billionaires’ family offices. This is not investment attraction — it is a wealth shelter branded as economic development.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Forest City was a failed property project. Billions of dollars in Chinese investment produced a ghost city that Mahathir himself mocked. Instead of letting the market price in the failure, the Anwar government is using public policy — tax exemptions, regulatory incentives, infrastructure spending — to rescue a private development. The beneficiaries are property developers and ultra-wealthy investors. The cost is borne by Malaysian taxpayers who lose the revenue that tax-exempt businesses would otherwise contribute. Socialising losses and privatising gains is the definition of crony capitalism — and PH promised to end it.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a long history of special economic zones that promised transformation and delivered tax giveaways. The Iskandar Development Region offered tax incentives for over a decade — Johor’s inequality worsened. Labuan’s offshore financial centre attracted shell companies more than genuine investment. Cyberjaya was supposed to be Malaysia’s Silicon Valley — it became an office park. Each zone follows the same pattern: generous tax incentives, minimal clawback mechanisms, and benefits that accrue to capital rather than labour. Forest City SFZ adds another chapter to this pattern.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You’re offering 0% tax to billionaires’ family offices while charging 8% SST on the rakyat’s healthcare and education. You’re rescuing a failed Chinese property project with public policy incentives. You’re socialising the losses and privatising the gains — exactly what you accused BN of doing. Publish a cost-benefit analysis of the SFZ. Show how many Malaysian jobs it will create versus how much tax revenue Malaysia loses. Explain why ultra-wealthy family offices deserve 0% tax when hawkers pay SST. Or admit that the SFZ is a bailout for developers, not an economic strategy for Malaysia.

Sources: Nikkei Asia | Reuters | Straits Times

item.title: Diesel Price Jumps 56% Overnight — Anwar Says Critics Will Attack Him Regardless
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-06-10

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 10 June 2024, diesel prices in Peninsular Malaysia jumped from RM2.15 to RM3.35 per litre — a 56% increase overnight. Finance Minister II Amir Hamzah Azizan announced the price float on 9 June, effective the next morning — one day’s notice for a shock that rippled through logistics, food prices, and small businesses. Sabah, Sarawak, and Labuan remained at RM2.15. Anwar told reporters: “We must also know that whatever we do, we will be severely criticised with all sorts of slander and lies.” His response to citizens facing a 56% cost increase was to complain about the quality of criticism he was receiving.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The diesel subsidy bill had ballooned to RM14.3 billion annually, and most economists agreed the blanket subsidy was regressive. The policy may be economically necessary, but the execution was politically negligent. A 56% price hike with one day’s notice is a shock to households, small businesses, fishermen, and farmers who cannot absorb overnight cost increases. Phased implementation over 6-12 months would have achieved the same fiscal goal with a fraction of the pain. The Diplomat noted that diesel increases have cascading effects — logistics, food transport, construction, agriculture, and fishing all depend on diesel.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar’s dismissal of all criticism as “slander and lies” is not just tone-deaf — it is contemptuous. Many of the people struggling with higher diesel costs are PH voters. They are not slandering the PM — they are telling him they cannot afford to fill their trucks. The two-tier pricing system created an immediate arbitrage problem — diesel smuggling from Sabah and Sarawak increased, adding enforcement costs. The government promised RM14.3 billion in savings would fund public transport and targeted aid. But PADU was sidelined. Public transport expansion outside KL remains negligible. The money was saved, but the promised reinvestment is largely invisible to the people who sacrificed.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: When Najib attempted fuel subsidy reform in 2014, PH was among the loudest critics. When petrol prices fluctuated under the managed float system, PH accused BN of burdening the people. Now in government, PH implemented an even more aggressive subsidy cut — 56% overnight versus Najib’s gradual float — and called critics slanderers. The hypocrisy of implementing tougher measures than those you attacked your predecessors for is the defining feature of PH’s economic governance.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You raised diesel by 56% overnight with one day’s notice. Your response to criticism was to call it slander. Your promised reinvestment — public transport, targeted aid — hasn’t materialised. PADU was sidelined. Buses didn’t appear. If the rakyat must tighten their belts, show them you’re tightening yours first. Publish where the RM14.3 billion in savings went. Deliver the public transport improvements you promised. Phase future subsidy reforms gradually. Or keep imposing overnight shocks and dismissing the pain as slander.

Sources: Reuters | UCA News | The Diplomat

item.title: Diesel Price Jumps 56% Overnight as PH Removes Subsidies — Rakyat Told to 'Tighten Belts'
item.description:
Said by: Amir Hamzah Azizan, Finance Minister II; Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-06-10

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 10 June 2024, the PH government removed diesel subsidies in Peninsular Malaysia, causing the price to jump from RM2.15 to RM3.35 per litre overnight — a 56% increase. Finance Minister II Amir Hamzah Azizan framed it as “targeted subsidy rationalisation” and announced a Subsidised Diesel Control System for eligible sectors. The impact was immediate: transport costs surged, food prices climbed, and businesses passed the costs on to consumers. The government promised B40 and M40 households would be protected through BUDI MADANI cash assistance, but payments were widely criticised as insufficient.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Subsidy reform is necessary — Malaysia was spending RM14.3 billion on diesel subsidies in 2023, much of which leaked to smuggling and foreign vehicles. The fiscal case is strong. But the execution was brutal. A 56% overnight price increase hits working Malaysians hard, especially in logistics-dependent sectors. Research showed businesses across multiple industries faced immediate cost pressures, passing costs directly to consumers — exactly the outcome PH promised to prevent. Sabah and Sarawak were exempted, creating a two-tier system that was as much about coalition management as regional sensitivity.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH spent years attacking BN’s subsidy policies — both for being too generous (fiscal waste) and for any attempt to reform them (burdening the rakyat). Classic opposition have-your-cake-and-eat-it. Now in power, PH removed diesel subsidies with a 56% overnight hike and told Malaysians it was for their own good. The BUDI MADANI cash assistance was modest compared to cascading cost increases. The savings were supposed to fund public transport and targeted transfers. By 2026, public transport outside KL remains minimal, and PADU — the targeting mechanism — was sidelined. The money was saved. The reinvestment was not delivered.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: When Najib attempted fuel subsidy reform in 2014, PH was among the loudest critics. The broader pattern is consistent: PH promises populist policies in opposition, then implements the same tough economic measures in government that they attacked their predecessors for. Subsidy reform, GST (abolished, replaced with less efficient SST), and toll abolition (still hasn’t happened) all follow the same trajectory. AMRO’s analysis recommended a phased approach — PH chose shock therapy instead.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Subsidy reform was necessary. Nobody disputes that. But you could have done it gradually, transparently, and with adequate cushioning. Instead, you did it fast and hard, while your ministers still enjoy subsidised fuel cards. Publish where the RM14.3 billion in annual savings went. Deliver the public transport improvements you promised. If the rakyat must tighten their belts, show them you’re tightening yours first. Or keep implementing overnight shocks while promising reinvestment that never arrives.

Sources: MOF Malaysia | Fulcrum | LSE | AMRO

item.title: Dual 5G Network: Billions Wasted on Political Compromise — One Network Would Have Sufficed
item.description:
Said by: Telecom Analysts / Consumers

Category: Economy

Date: 2024-06-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In mid-2024, the PH government shifted Malaysia’s 5G rollout from a single wholesale network (Digital Nasional Berhad, DNB) to a dual-network model — allowing a second 5G network to be built alongside DNB. The move was widely seen as a political compromise to accommodate major telecom operators with political connections who had opposed the single-network model. The dual-network approach doubles infrastructure costs, splits spectrum efficiency, and delays rural coverage expansion — all to solve a political problem, not a technical one.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: 5G infrastructure costs billions.
